[2026-08-04 20:27:02 INFO] Process design reviewer: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department.
process text: 
<process>
  <activity role='ED triage nurse' action='triage patient as suspected TIA or minor stroke' objects='patient presentation information' id='activity1_1'/>
  <activity role='ED team' action='assign urgent stroke priority and alert the ED team' objects='stroke priority status and team notification' id='activity1_2'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='measure random blood glucose on presentation' objects='blood glucose sample' id='activity2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='first random glucose indicates hypoglycemia' id='branch2'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='correct hypoglycemia immediately' objects='hypoglycemia treatment' id='activity3'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='first random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L' id='branch3'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='repeat blood glucose measurement' objects='blood glucose sample' id='activity4'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='first random glucose is not hypoglycemic and is 11 mmol/L or less' id='branch4'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='document glucose result and continue routine monitoring' objects='blood glucose result' id='activity5'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch12'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='assess symptom onset time and symptom pattern' objects='clinical history and neurologic findings' id='activity13_1'/>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='assess high-risk features and minor stroke status' objects='risk features and stroke severity findings' id='activity13_2'/>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='perform swallowing screening' objects='swallowing screening result' id='activity13_3'/>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='review current antiplatelet use' objects='medication history' id='activity13_4'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='patient has TIA with fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty' id='branch13'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='evaluate patient in the ED to consider admission to hospital' objects='ED assessment and admission criteria' id='activity14'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient does not have fluctuating or crescendo TIA symptoms requiring ED admission consideration' id='branch14'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='continue risk-stratified TIA or minor stroke pathway' objects='risk assessment result' id='activity15'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
        <branch condition='patient has TIA with high-risk features or minor nondisabling stroke' id='branch15'>
          <activity role='stroke clinician' action='prioritize urgent high-risk TIA or minor stroke management' objects='risk classification' id='activity16'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5'>
            <branch condition='medical attention is sought within 48 hours of symptom onset' id='branch16'>
              <activity role='stroke clinician' action='select the safest location for immediate evaluation' objects='access to specialized clinic, patient risk status' id='activity17'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6'>
                <branch condition='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic is accessible without risky delay' id='branch17'>
                  <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='provide immediate care at a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic' objects='clinic stroke expertise and rapid investigation resources' id='activity18'/>
                  <activity role='stroke team' action='perform brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity20'/>
                  <activity role='stroke clinician' action='exclude intracranial hemorrhage on brain imaging' objects='brain CT result' id='activity21'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway7'>
                    <branch condition='patient is not on an antiplatelet agent at presentation, antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated, and intracranial hemorrhage is excluded' id='branch19'>
                      <activity role='stroke clinician' action='select the immediate antiplatelet regimen' objects='antiplatelet treatment options' id='activity22'/>
                      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway8'>
                        <branch condition='ASA regimen is selected' id='branch20'>
                          <activity role='stroke clinician' action='start ASA 160 mg loading dose followed by enteric coated ASA 81 to 325 mg daily, with most patients maintained on 81 mg per day' objects='ASA regimen' id='activity23'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='clopidogrel regimen is selected' id='branch21'>
                          <activity role='stroke clinician' action='start clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose followed by 75 mg per day' objects='clopidogrel regimen' id='activity24'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA regimen is selected' id='branch22'>
                          <activity role='stroke clinician' action='load with ASA 160 mg first and then start extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg plus ASA 25 mg twice daily' objects='extended-release dipyridamole and ASA regimen' id='activity25'/>
                        </branch>
                      </exclusiveGateway>
                      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway9'>
                        <branch condition='ASA is required and the patient fails swallowing screening or cannot swallow' id='branch23'>
                          <activity role='ED team' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA as necessary' objects='rectal ASA or gastric ASA' id='activity26'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='ASA is not required or the patient can swallow' id='branch24'>
                          <activity role='stroke clinician' action='continue the selected antiplatelet regimen' objects='selected antiplatelet therapy' id='activity27'/>
                        </branch>
                      </exclusiveGateway>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='patient is already on an antiplatelet agent at presentation or antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated or intracranial hemorrhage is not excluded' id='branch25'>
                      <activity role='stroke clinician' action='document current antiplatelet status or contraindication and tailor the stroke-prevention plan accordingly' objects='medication history, contraindications, imaging result' id='activity28'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                  <activity role='stroke clinician' action='initiate other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to the patient' objects='tailored stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity29'/>
                  <activity role='stroke or clinic team' action='assess cross-continuum secondary prevention needs for ambulatory patient' objects='secondary prevention risk profile and ambulatory care setting needs' id='activity54_1a'/>
                  <activity role='stroke or clinic team' action='initiate or reconcile appropriate secondary prevention therapies' objects='secondary prevention medications and nonpharmacologic interventions' id='activity54_2a'/>
                  <activity role='stroke or clinic team' action='communicate follow-up plan, referrals, and patient instructions across care settings' objects='transition plan, referrals, patient education' id='activity54_3a'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='delay to specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic poses risk to patient health' id='branch18'>
                  <activity role='appropriately resourced ED team' action='perform urgent ED diagnostic evaluation' objects='ED stroke resources and diagnostic workup' id='activity19_1'/>
                  <activity role='ED clinician' action='assess need for inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention' objects='admission criteria and clinical risk status' id='activity19_2'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6_1'>
                    <branch condition='inpatient evaluation and management is indicated' id='branch18_1'>
                      <activity role='ED clinician' action='admit patient for inpatient stroke-prevention evaluation and management' objects='admission order and inpatient management plan' id='activity19_3'/>
                      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway3'>
                        <branch id='branch44'>

suggestion:
Problem: In the branch with id='branch13' under the exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway3', the activity with id='activity14' ("evaluate patient in the ED to consider admission to hospital") does not lead to any actual admission decision. After this activity, the process still continues into the general risk-stratification gateway with id='exclusivegateway4', which means a patient with fluctuating or crescendo symptoms could still be routed into an outpatient pathway without a clear admission outcome.

Suggested modification: After the activity with id='activity14', add a new exclusive gateway to capture the result of the admission consideration:
- one branch with condition='admission to hospital is indicated' leading directly to the inpatient management path (for example, to an admission activity like id='activity19_3' or an equivalent admitted-patient pathway),
- one branch with condition='admission to hospital is not indicated' leading to the usual risk-stratified pathway (for example, to id='activity15' or directly to the gateway with id='exclusivegateway4').

Reason: Recommendation 2.4.2 specifically says that these TIA patients should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital. In the current design, that evaluation is performed, but its outcome is never used to control the flow. This creates a logic gap and weakens the purpose of the gateway branch.
You need to modify the process text according to the suggestions and output it.
Be careful not to change the format:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

[2026-08-04 20:29:49 INFO] Process design expert: **Process design expert<->Process design reviewer on : ProcessReview, turn 0**

[You're a process design reviewer. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include give suggestion to the process.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

| **task_prompt** | Module 2: Early Treatment This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). Module 2 Recommended Practices  2.1 Glucose management 2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately 2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first random glucose value is &gt; 11 mmol/L 2.2 Body temperature 2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours For temperature &gt; 37.5 C: • initiate temperature-reducing measures • investigate potential infection • initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required 2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed: • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high- risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset, followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends that: o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinicc o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of the location of initial presentation o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial presentation • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation, followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset 2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital 2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage: • ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81– 325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of 81 mg/day • clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day • extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with ASA 160 mg first) 2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as for patients who fail swallowing screening 2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery atherosclerosis with &gt; 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event. bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests. dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus. eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 2 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED • Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department. |

best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The\nmajority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital\nand should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or\ncomparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.\nbPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging\ncA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and\nvascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.\ndFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.\neMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or\narm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.\n\nThe following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.\nModule 2 Implementation Considerations\nGeneral considerations\n• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as\npossible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED\n• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA\nAbbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department.', 'suggestions': 'Problem: In the branch with id=\'branch13\' under the exclusive gateway with id=\'exclusivegateway3\', the activity with id=\'activity14\' ("evaluate patient in the ED to consider admission to hospital") does not lead to any actual admission decision. After this activity, the process still continues into the general risk-stratification gateway with id=\'exclusivegateway4\', which means a patient with fluctuating or crescendo symptoms could still be routed into an outpatient pathway without a clear admission outcome.\n\nSuggested modification: After the activity with id=\'activity14\', add a new exclusive gateway to capture the result of the admission consideration:\n- one branch with condition=\'admission to hospital is indicated\' leading directly to the inpatient management path (for example, to an admission activity like id=\'activity19_3\' or an equivalent admitted-patient pathway),\n- one branch with condition=\'admission to hospital is not indicated\' leading to the usual risk-stratified pathway (for example, to id=\'activity15\' or directly to the gateway with id=\'exclusivegateway4\').\n\nReason: Recommendation 2.4.2 specifically says that these TIA patients should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital. In the current design, that evaluation is performed, but its outcome is never used to control the flow. | **task_prompt** | Module 2: Early Treatment This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). Module 2 Recommended Practices  2.1 Glucose management 2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately 2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first random glucose value is &gt; 11 mmol/L 2.2 Body temperature 2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours For temperature &gt; 37.5 C: • initiate temperature-reducing measures • investigate potential infection • initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required 2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed: • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high- risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset, followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends that: o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinicc o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of the location of initial presentation o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial presentation • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation, followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset 2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital 2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage: • ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81– 325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of 81 mg/day • clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day • extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with ASA 160 mg first) 2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as for patients who fail swallowing screening 2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery atherosclerosis with &gt; 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event. bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests. dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus. eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 2 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED • Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department. |

After this activity, the process still continues into the general risk-stratification gateway with id=\'exclusivegateway4\', which means a patient with fluctuating or crescendo symptoms could still be routed into an outpatient pathway without a clear admission outcome.\n\nSuggested modification: After the activity with id=\'activity14\', add a new exclusive gateway to capture the result of the admission consideration:\n- one branch with condition=\'admission to hospital is indicated\' leading directly to the inpatient management path (for example, to an admission activity like id=\'activity19_3\' or an equivalent admitted-patient pathway),\n- one branch with condition=\'admission to hospital is not indicated\' leading to the usual risk-stratified pathway (for example, to id=\'activity15\' or directly to the gateway with id=\'exclusivegateway4\').\n\nReason: Recommendation 2.4.2 specifically says that these TIA patients should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital. In the current design, that evaluation is performed, but its outcome is never used to control the flow. This creates a logic gap and weakens the purpose of the gateway branch.', 'format': "\nWhen the process starts, please output ''\nAn activity is represented as follows:\n\nThe exclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe parallel gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe inclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nAt the end of the process, please output ''\n\nThere are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:\n    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.\n    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.\n    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.\n    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.\n    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.\n    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.\n    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.\n    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.\n", 'examples': ' '} |

[2026-08-04 20:29:49 INFO] Team leader: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design reviewer. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include give suggestion to the process.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department.
process text:
<process>
  <activity role='ED triage nurse' action='triage patient as suspected TIA or minor stroke' objects='patient presentation information' id='activity1_1'/>
  <activity role='ED team' action='assign urgent stroke priority and alert the ED team' objects='stroke priority status and team notification' id='activity1_2'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='measure random blood glucose on presentation' objects='blood glucose sample' id='activity2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='first random glucose indicates hypoglycemia' id='branch2'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='correct hypoglycemia immediately' objects='hypoglycemia treatment' id='activity3'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='first random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L' id='branch3'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='repeat blood glucose measurement' objects='blood glucose sample' id='activity4'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='first random glucose is not hypoglycemic and is 11 mmol/L or less' id='branch4'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='document glucose result and continue routine monitoring' objects='blood glucose result' id='activity5'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch12'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='assess symptom onset time and symptom pattern' objects='clinical history and neurologic findings' id='activity13_1'/>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='assess high-risk features and minor stroke status' objects='risk features and stroke severity findings' id='activity13_2'/>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='perform swallowing screening' objects='swallowing screening result' id='activity13_3'/>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='review current antiplatelet use' objects='medication history' id='activity13_4'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='patient has TIA with fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty' id='branch13'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='evaluate patient in the ED to consider admission to hospital' objects='ED assessment and admission criteria' id='activity14'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway17'>
            <branch condition='admission to hospital is indicated' id='branch46'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='admit patient for inpatient stroke-prevention evaluation and management after ED admission consideration' objects='admission order and inpatient management plan' id='activity14_1'/>
              <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway4'>
                <branch id='branch46a'>
                  <activity role='ED or inpatient nurse' action='evaluate temperature as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for the first 48 hours' objects='temperature readings' id='activity6_a'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2_a'>
                    <branch condition='temperature is > 37.5 C' id='branch6_a'>
                      <activity role='ED or inpatient team' action='recognize fever that requires early treatment' objects='temperature reading' id='activity7_a'/>
                      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2_a'>
                        <branch id='branch7_a'>
                          <activity role='ED or inpatient team' action='initiate temperature-reducing measures' objects='temperature-reducing interventions' id='activity8_a'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch id='branch8_a'>
                          <activity role='ED or inpatient clinician' action='investigate potential infection' objects='infection assessment' id='activity9_a'/>
                        </branch>
                      </parallelGateway>
                      <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1_a'>
                        <branch condition='antipyretic therapy is required' id='branch9_a'>
                          <activity role='ED or inpatient clinician' action='initiate antipyretic therapy' objects='antipyretic medication' id='activity10_a'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='antimicrobial therapy is required' id='branch10_a'>
                          <activity role='ED or inpatient clinician' action='initiate antimicrobial therapy' objects='antimicrobial medication' id='activity11_a'/>
                        </branch>
                      </inclusiveGateway>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='temperature is 37.5 C or less' id='branch11_a'>
                      <activity role='ED or inpatient nurse' action='continue routine temperature monitoring every 4 hours for the first 48 hours' objects='temperature readings' id='activity12_a'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
                <branch id='branch46b'>
                  <activity role='stroke team' action='perform brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity20_b'/>
                  <activity role='stroke clinician' action='exclude intracranial hemorrhage on brain imaging' objects='brain CT result' id='activity21_b'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway7_b'>
                    <branch condition='patient is not on an antiplatelet agent at presentation, antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated, and intracranial hemorrhage is excluded' id='branch19_b'>
                      <activity role='stroke clinician' action='select the immediate antiplatelet regimen' objects='antiplatelet treatment options' id='activity22_b'/>
                      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway8_b'>
                        <branch condition='ASA regimen is selected' id='branch20_b'>
                          <activity role='stroke clinician' action='start ASA 160 mg loading dose followed by enteric coated ASA 81 to 325 mg daily, with most patients maintained on 81 mg per day' objects='ASA regimen' id='activity23_b'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='clopidogrel regimen is selected' id='branch21_b'>
                          <activity role='stroke clinician' action='start clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose followed by 75 mg per day' objects='clopidogrel regimen' id='activity24_b'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA regimen is selected' id='branch22_b'>
                          <activity role='stroke clinician' action='load with ASA 160 mg first and then start extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg plus ASA 25 mg twice daily' objects='extended-release dipyridamole and ASA regimen' id='activity25_b'/>
                        </branch>
                      </exclusiveGateway>
                      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway9_b'>
                        <branch condition='ASA is required and the patient fails swallowing screening or cannot swallow' id='branch23_b'>
                          <activity role='ED or inpatient team' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA as necessary' objects='rectal ASA or gastric ASA' id='activity26_b'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='ASA is not required or the patient can swallow' id='branch24_b'>
                          <activity role='stroke clinician' action='continue the selected antiplatelet regimen' objects='selected antiplatelet therapy' id='activity27_b'/>
                        </branch>
                      </exclusiveGateway>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='patient is already on an antiplatelet agent at presentation or antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated or intracranial hemorrhage is not excluded' id='branch25_b'>
                      <activity role='stroke clinician' action='document current antiplatelet status or contraindication and tailor the stroke-prevention plan accordingly' objects='medication history, contraindications, imaging result' id='activity28_b'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                  <activity role='stroke clinician' action='initiate other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to the patient' objects='tailored stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity29_b'/>
                  <activity role='stroke or inpatient team' action='assess cross-continuum secondary prevention needs for admitted patient' objects='secondary prevention risk profile and inpatient care setting needs' id='activity54_1f'/>
                  <activity role='stroke or inpatient team' action='initiate or reconcile appropriate secondary prevention therapies' objects='secondary prevention medications and nonpharmacologic interventions' id='activity54_2f'/>
                  <activity role='stroke or inpatient team' action='communicate follow-up plan, referrals, and patient instructions across care settings' objects='transition plan, referrals, patient education' id='activity54_3f'/>
                </branch>
              </parallelGateway>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='admission to hospital is not indicated' id='branch47'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='continue risk-stratified TIA or minor stroke pathway after admission is not indicated' objects='risk assessment result' id='activity15_1'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4_1'>
                <branch condition='patient has TIA with high-risk features or minor nondisabling stroke' id='branch15_a'>
                  <activity role='stroke clinician' action='prioritize urgent high-risk TIA or minor stroke management' objects='risk classification' id='activity16_a'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5_a'>
                    <branch condition='medical attention is sought within 48 hours of symptom onset' id='branch16_a'>
                      <activity role='stroke clinician' action='select the safest location for immediate evaluation' objects='access to specialized clinic, patient risk status' id='activity17_a'/>
                      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6_a'>
                        <branch condition='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic is accessible without risky delay' id='branch17_a'>
                          <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='provide immediate care at a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic' objects='clinic stroke expertise and rapid investigation resources' id='activity18_a'/>
                          <activity role='stroke team' action='perform brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity20_a'/>

                          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway7_a'>
                            <branch condition='patient is not on an antiplatelet agent at presentation, antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated, and intracranial hemorrhage is excluded' id='branch19_a'>

                              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway8_a'>

                          <activity role='stroke or ED team' action='assess cross-continuum secondary prevention needs for discharged patient' objects='secondary prevention risk profile and discharge care setting needs' id='activity54_1c'/>
                          <activity role='stroke or ED team' action='initiate or reconcile appropriate secondary prevention therapies' objects='secondary prevention medications and nonpharmacologic interventions' id='activity54_2c'/>
                          <activity role='stroke or ED team' action='communicate follow-up plan, referrals, and patient instructions across care settings' objects='transition plan, referrals, patient education' id='activity54_3c'/>
                        </branch>
                      </exclusiveGateway>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='medical attention is sought more than 48 hours after symptom onset' id='branch26'>
                  <activity role='stroke clinician' action='arrange referral within 24 hours of initial presentation for urgent evaluation' objects='referral request' id='activity30'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway10'>
                    <branch condition='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic is selected for referral' id='branch27'>
                      <activity role='referral coordinator' action='refer patient to a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic within 24 hours of initial presentation' objects='specialized clinic referral' id='activity31'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services is selected for referral' id='branch28'>
                      <activity role='referral coordinator' action='refer patient to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services within 24 hours of initial presentation' objects='outpatient stroke-prevention clinic referral' id='activity32'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                  <activity role='receiving stroke service' action='perform evaluation including brain imaging after referral' objects='diagnostic evaluation and brain imaging' id='activity33'/>
                  <activity role='stroke clinician' action='exclude intracranial hemorrhage on brain imaging' objects='brain imaging result' id='activity34'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway11'>
                    <branch condition='patient is not on an antiplatelet agent at presentation, antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated, and intracranial hemorrhage is excluded' id='branch29'>
                      <activity role='stroke clinician' action='select the immediate antiplatelet regimen' objects='antiplatelet treatment options' id='activity35'/>
                      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway12'>
                        <branch condition='ASA regimen is selected' id='branch30'>

                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='clopidogrel regimen is selected' id='branch31'>

                        </branch>

                        </branch>
                      </exclusiveGateway>
                      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway13'>

                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='ASA is not required or the patient can swallow' id='branch34'>
                          <activity role='stroke clinician' action='continue the selected antiplatelet regimen' objects='selected antiplatelet therapy' id='activity40'/>
                        </branch>
                      </exclusiveGateway>
                    </branch>

                    </branch>

                  <activity role='stroke clinician' action='initiate other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to the patient' objects='tailored stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity42'/>
